source: NCERT-aligned question bank for SAH microtests (local fallback)

chapters.subject: Science
chapters.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

chapters.subject: Science
chapters.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Which of these is most important in science?

options.A: Guessing without checking
options.B: Copying answers only
options.C: Observing carefully and using evidence

questions.answer: C
questions.answerSolution: C
questions.explanation: Science depends on observation and evidence.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Nature of science
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A science teacher uses a ball-and-stick model to explain molecules. What is the main purpose of the model?

options.A: To show the exact size of every atom
options.B: To represent a complex idea in a simpler form
options.C: To prove that molecules are visible to the eye

questions.answer: B
questions.answerSolution: B
questions.explanation: Models simplify complex structures so they can be studied and discussed.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains the purpose of scientific models.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Why do scientists use standard units and symbols?

options.A: To communicate measurements clearly and consistently
options.B: To make science more difficult
options.C: To avoid doing calculations

questions.answer: A
questions.answerSolution: A
questions.explanation: Standard units and symbols reduce confusion and make communication clear across places.
questions.learningOutcome: Understands the need for standard scientific communication.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A student records plant height every two days and plots a graph. What is the graph mainly helping the student identify?

options.A: The colour of the plant
options.B: The exact age of the plant
options.C: The name of the plant species
options.D: The pattern of growth over time

questions.answer: D
questions.answerSolution: D
questions.explanation: Graphs help identify patterns and trends in data.
questions.learningOutcome: Connects mathematical representation with scientific interpretation.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Mathematics in science
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Which statement can be tested by doing an experiment?

options.A: This plant is lucky
options.B: Plants grow taller when they get enough water
options.C: This plant looks happy

questions.answer: B
questions.answerSolution: B
questions.explanation: The effect of water on plant growth can be tested by giving different amounts of water and measuring growth.
questions.learningOutcome: Distinguishes testable scientific statements from vague claims.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A map of India is much smaller than the real country. Why is it still useful?

options.A: It shows the real size of India
options.B: It is useless because it is small
options.C: It represents important features in a simple way
options.D: It proves that India is flat

questions.answer: C
questions.answerSolution: C
questions.explanation: A map is a simplified model that helps us understand locations.
questions.learningOutcome: Identifies simple models from daily life.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A student changes her answer after seeing better evidence from an experiment. This shows that she is:

options.A: Thinking scientifically
options.B: Always wrong
options.C: Ignoring observations

questions.answer: A
questions.answerSolution: A
questions.explanation: Scientific thinking improves conclusions when better evidence is available.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A farmer studies soil, rainfall, and plant growth before sowing seeds. This shows that science often uses:

options.A: Only one idea at a time
options.B: Only drawing
options.C: Only memorisation

questions.answer: D
questions.answerSolution: D
questions.explanation: Real-life science can use ideas from different areas together.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Assertion (A): A model of the human body can help students learn organs. Reason (R): A model can show important parts in a simple way. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: A
questions.answerSolution: A
questions.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why the model is useful.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Assertion (A): A conclusion based on repeated observations is more reliable than one based on a single observation. Reason (R): Repeated observations can reduce the effect of accidental errors. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: A
questions.answerSolution: A
questions.explanation: Repeated observations help reduce accidental error, making conclusions more reliable.
questions.learningOutcome: Connects repetition with reliability of evidence.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Assertion (A): Standard units are needed in science. Reason (R): Without standard units, the same measurement may be understood differently by different people. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: A
questions.answerSolution: A
questions.explanation: Standard units avoid confusion in measurement and communication.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains the role of standard units.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Assertion (A): A claim should be checked before accepting it as scientific. Reason (R): Scientific claims are supported by evidence. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: A
questions.answerSolution: A
questions.explanation: Both statements are true, and evidence is the reason a claim can be accepted scientifically.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: What is a scientific model? Give one example.
questions.answer: A scientific model is a simple representation of something. Example: a globe is a model of Earth.
questions.answerSolution: A scientific model is a simple representation of something. Example: a globe is a model of Earth.
questions.explanation: The answer should define model and give a familiar example.
questions.learningOutcome: States the function of models in science.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A globe does not show every street and house on Earth. Does this make the globe useless? Give a reason.
questions.answer: No. It is still useful because it shows the shape of Earth and positions of large places, even though it leaves out many details.
questions.answerSolution: No. It is still useful because it shows the shape of Earth and positions of large places, even though it leaves out many details.
questions.explanation: The answer should show that models can be useful even when incomplete.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains why assumptions and limits matter in models.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: How do symbols and units make scientific communication easier?
questions.answer: They provide a common, concise language so measurements and ideas can be understood in the same way by everyone.
questions.answerSolution: They provide a common, concise language so measurements and ideas can be understood in the same way by everyone.
questions.explanation: Symbols and units create clarity and consistency.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains the need for common scientific language.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A student says, 'The metal became hot because heat flowed into it.' Identify the observation and the inference.
questions.answer: Observation: the metal became hot. Inference: heat flowed into it.
questions.answerSolution: Observation: the metal became hot. Inference: heat flowed into it.
questions.explanation: Observation is what is directly noticed; inference is the explanation drawn from it.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Why is estimation useful before doing exact calculations in science?
questions.answer: Estimation helps check whether an answer is reasonable and gives a quick approximate idea of the result.
questions.answerSolution: Estimation helps check whether an answer is reasonable and gives a quick approximate idea of the result.
questions.explanation: Estimation supports error checking and planning.
questions.learningOutcome: Recognises estimation as a scientific tool.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Mathematics in science
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Why may a scientist repeat an experiment more than once?
questions.answer: Repeating an experiment helps check whether the result is reliable and not due to a mistake or chance.
questions.answerSolution: Repeating an experiment helps check whether the result is reliable and not due to a mistake or chance.
questions.explanation: Repetition improves confidence in results.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A globe is used as a model of Earth. Mention one feature it represents well and two features it cannot show accurately.
questions.answer: It represents Earth's spherical shape and relative positions of continents. It cannot show the exact surface details of every place or the real size of Earth in actual scale.
questions.answerSolution: It represents Earth's spherical shape and relative positions of continents. It cannot show the exact surface details of every place or the real size of Earth in actual scale.
questions.explanation: Good answers should show both usefulness and limitation of a model.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A student claims that plants grow faster when classical music is played. Write three points that would make this claim scientifically testable.
questions.answer: Use similar plants, keep conditions like water and light the same, compare a music group with a no-music group, and measure growth over time.
questions.answerSolution: Use similar plants, keep conditions like water and light the same, compare a music group with a no-music group, and measure growth over time.
questions.explanation: The answer should include control of variables, comparison, and measurable evidence.
questions.learningOutcome: Applies testability to a real-life claim.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Why is a table or graph often better than a paragraph for presenting repeated measurements?
questions.answer: A table organises exact values clearly, while a graph shows patterns, trends, and comparisons quickly.
questions.answerSolution: A table organises exact values clearly, while a graph shows patterns, trends, and comparisons quickly.
questions.explanation: Data representation helps interpretation.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains the role of mathematical tools in science.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Mathematics in science
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A shopkeeper says a packet is 'heavy', while a scientist says it has a mass of 2 kg. Why is the scientific statement better?
questions.answer: The scientific statement is measurable, uses a standard unit, and can be compared by anyone. 'Heavy' is subjective.
questions.answerSolution: The scientific statement is measurable, uses a standard unit, and can be compared by anyone. 'Heavy' is subjective.
questions.explanation: Scientific language reduces ambiguity.
questions.learningOutcome: Compares everyday language with scientific language.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A student first thinks that a heavier ball always falls faster. After doing an experiment, both balls reach almost together. What should the student do and why?
questions.answer: The student should change the earlier idea because the experiment gives better evidence. In science, explanations should be based on observations and evidence.
questions.answerSolution: The student should change the earlier idea because the experiment gives better evidence. In science, explanations should be based on observations and evidence.
questions.explanation: The question checks willingness to revise ideas after evidence.
questions.learningOutcome: Understands the role of evidence in changing ideas.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A farmer wants better crop growth. How can knowledge of soil, water, and weather help the farmer?
questions.answer: Soil knowledge helps choose suitable nutrients, water knowledge helps decide irrigation, and weather knowledge helps decide sowing time and crop care.
questions.answerSolution: Soil knowledge helps choose suitable nutrients, water knowledge helps decide irrigation, and weather knowledge helps decide sowing time and crop care.
questions.explanation: This keeps interdisciplinary thinking in a familiar context.
questions.learningOutcome: Applies scientific thinking to daily life.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A group of students tested whether adding compost affects plant growth. They used two groups of same-sized plants. Group A received compost and Group B did not. Both groups got equal sunlight and water for 20 days. (a) Identify the variable changed by students. (b) Why was Group B needed? (c) Name two conditions kept same. (d) What data should be recorded?
questions.answer: (a) Compost added. (b) It is the comparison/control group. (c) Sunlight and water, also plant type or pot size. (d) Plant height or number of leaves over time.
questions.answerSolution: (a) Compost added. (b) It is the comparison/control group. (c) Sunlight and water, also plant type or pot size. (d) Plant height or number of leaves over time.
questions.explanation: This is a board-style case question testing variables, control and data.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A teacher uses a globe to explain Earth. Students can see continents and oceans on it, but not their school building or every road. (a) What does the globe represent? (b) Give one useful feature of this model. (c) Give one limitation. (d) Why is the globe still useful?
questions.answer: (a) Earth. (b) It shows the shape of Earth and positions of continents and oceans. (c) It cannot show every small detail. (d) It helps us understand Earth in a simple way.
questions.answerSolution: (a) Earth. (b) It shows the shape of Earth and positions of continents and oceans. (c) It cannot show every small detail. (d) It helps us understand Earth in a simple way.
questions.explanation: The question tests interpretation of a familiar model.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A student measured the temperature of water every minute while heating it and noticed that the values increased steadily at first. (a) Which tool can best show the trend? (b) Why are repeated readings useful? (c) What should the student do if one value is very different from nearby values? (d) Why is recording units necessary?
questions.answer: (a) A line graph. (b) They show the pattern and reduce reliance on one reading. (c) Recheck the measurement and method; do not ignore it without reason. (d) Units make the measurement clear and comparable.
questions.answerSolution: (a) A line graph. (b) They show the pattern and reduce reliance on one reading. (c) Recheck the measurement and method; do not ignore it without reason. (d) Units make the measurement clear and comparable.
questions.explanation: The question uses data-handling style common in board papers.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Mathematics in science
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Explain how a scientific investigation moves from a question to a reliable conclusion. Use an example from daily life.
questions.answer: A good answer should include asking a testable question, making a hypothesis, planning a fair test, collecting observations or measurements, analysing data, and drawing a conclusion. Example: testing which cloth dries fastest by keeping size, water amount, and sunlight same while measuring drying time.
questions.answerSolution: A good answer should include asking a testable question, making a hypothesis, planning a fair test, collecting observations or measurements, analysing data, and drawing a conclusion. Example: testing which cloth dries fastest by keeping size, water amount, and sunlight same while measuring drying time.
questions.explanation: Long-answer responses should be structured and example-based.
questions.learningOutcome: Describes the process of scientific investigation.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Nature of science
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Explain the usefulness and limitation of any two models used in science or daily life.
questions.answer: Example 1: A globe helps us understand Earth's shape and large places, but cannot show every small detail. Example 2: A human body model helps locate organs, but does not show all real body functions. Models are useful because they simplify things, but their limits must be remembered.
questions.answerSolution: Example 1: A globe helps us understand Earth's shape and large places, but cannot show every small detail. Example 2: A human body model helps locate organs, but does not show all real body functions. Models are useful because they simplify things, but their limits must be remembered.
questions.explanation: The answer must balance usefulness and limitation using familiar examples.
questions.learningOutcome: Justifies the value and limits of scientific models.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: A student says, 'Science becomes reliable because it uses evidence, units, and mathematics.' Explain this statement with examples.
questions.answer: Evidence helps us check whether an idea is correct. Units such as metre, kilogram and second make measurements clear. Mathematics helps organise data in tables, compare values and draw graphs. For example, measuring plant growth in centimetres every week gives clearer information than simply saying the plant grew a lot.
questions.answerSolution: Evidence helps us check whether an idea is correct. Units such as metre, kilogram and second make measurements clear. Mathematics helps organise data in tables, compare values and draw graphs. For example, measuring plant growth in centimetres every week gives clearer information than simply saying the plant grew a lot.
questions.explanation: This integrates chapter themes with simple examples.
questions.learningOutcome: Connects evidence, measurement and mathematics in science.
questions.notes: Board-style practice question for Class 9 foundation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Suppose you ride a bicycle from your school to your home. You want to model the time it takes to go home from school. What details would you keep? What details could you ignore? Suggest why ignoring some details may actually be useful.
questions.answer: Keep details such as distance, average speed, traffic stops, road slope, and time spent at signals. Ignore details such as bicycle colour, brand, clothes worn, or small changes in wind if making a simple first model. Ignoring less important details keeps the model simple and helps focus on the main factors affecting time.
questions.answerSolution: Keep details such as distance, average speed, traffic stops, road slope, and time spent at signals. Ignore details such as bicycle colour, brand, clothes worn, or small changes in wind if making a simple first model. Ignoring less important details keeps the model simple and helps focus on the main factors affecting time.
questions.explanation: Indicative answer for teacher reference; student responses may vary.
questions.learningOutcome: Answers NCERT textbook prompt using scientific reasoning.
questions.notes: Direct NCERT question from Activity/Pause and Ponder.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Think of a prediction you or your family made recently, for example the outcome of a cricket match. Was it based on evidence and reasoning, or mainly on guesswork? How can scientific thinking improve such predictions?
questions.answer: A prediction based only on liking a team is guesswork. A better prediction may use evidence such as recent performance, pitch, weather, player fitness, and past records. Scientific thinking improves predictions by using measurable information and checking patterns instead of relying only on feelings.
questions.answerSolution: A prediction based only on liking a team is guesswork. A better prediction may use evidence such as recent performance, pitch, weather, player fitness, and past records. Scientific thinking improves predictions by using measurable information and checking patterns instead of relying only on feelings.
questions.explanation: Indicative answer for teacher reference; student responses may vary.
questions.learningOutcome: Answers NCERT textbook prompt using scientific reasoning.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Pause and Ponder 1
questions.notes: Direct NCERT question from Activity/Pause and Ponder.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Describe one situation where an approximate answer is good enough, and one where you would need a very exact value.
questions.answer: An approximate answer is good enough when estimating how much rice a family may need for a month. An exact value is needed when giving the correct dose of medicine or measuring chemicals in a laboratory.
questions.answerSolution: An approximate answer is good enough when estimating how much rice a family may need for a month. An exact value is needed when giving the correct dose of medicine or measuring chemicals in a laboratory.
questions.explanation: Indicative answer for teacher reference; student responses may vary.
questions.learningOutcome: Answers NCERT textbook prompt using scientific reasoning.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Pause and Ponder 2
questions.notes: Direct NCERT question from Activity/Pause and Ponder.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science
questions.question: Choose a real-life object, maybe a pressure cooker or a mobile phone, or a problem, maybe a traffic jam near your school. Make a sketch listing what kind of ideas from physics, chemistry, biology, earth science, or mathematics are involved. Show how at least two branches of science connect with your example.
questions.answer: Example: For a pressure cooker, physics is involved in pressure, heat, and steam; chemistry is involved in changes during cooking; mathematics is involved in time, temperature, and quantity estimation. These branches connect because pressure and heat affect how food cooks, and measurements help use the cooker safely and efficiently.
questions.answerSolution: Example: For a pressure cooker, physics is involved in pressure, heat, and steam; chemistry is involved in changes during cooking; mathematics is involved in time, temperature, and quantity estimation. These branches connect because pressure and heat affect how food cooks, and measurements help use the cooker safely and efficiently.
questions.explanation: Indicative answer for teacher reference; student responses may vary.
questions.learningOutcome: Answers NCERT textbook prompt using scientific reasoning.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 1, Pause and Ponder 3
questions.notes: Direct NCERT question from Activity/Pause and Ponder.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Which statement best describes a cell?

options.A: The basic unit of life
options.B: A type of tissue only found in animals
options.C: A non-living particle
options.D: A part found only in plants

questions.answer: A cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life.
questions.answerSolution: A cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life.
questions.explanation: All living organisms are made of cells.
questions.learningOutcome: Identifies cell as basic unit of life.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why do we usually need a microscope to study cells?

options.A: Cells are usually too small to see clearly with the unaided eye
options.B: Cells are always transparent
options.C: Cells are found only in water

questions.answer: Cells are generally smaller than what the unaided eye can resolve.
questions.answerSolution: Cells are generally smaller than what the unaided eye can resolve.
questions.explanation: Microscopes magnify small structures and improve visibility.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains why microscopes are used to study cells.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Who first observed small box-like compartments in cork and named them cells?

options.A: Robert Hooke
options.B: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek
options.C: J. Craig Venter

questions.answer: Robert Hooke observed cork under a microscope and named the compartments cells.
questions.answerSolution: Robert Hooke observed cork under a microscope and named the compartments cells.
questions.explanation: The question checks a direct historical point from the chapter.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: If an eyepiece is 10X and the objective lens is 10X, what is the total magnification?
questions.answer: Total magnification = eyepiece magnification × objective magnification = 10 × 10 = 100X.
questions.answerSolution: Total magnification = eyepiece magnification × objective magnification = 10 × 10 = 100X.
questions.explanation: Uses the NCERT microscope example.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: The cell membrane is called selectively permeable because it:

options.A: allows some substances to pass and blocks others
options.B: allows everything to pass freely
options.C: does not allow water to pass

questions.answer: A selectively permeable membrane permits selected substances to move across it.
questions.answerSolution: A selectively permeable membrane permits selected substances to move across it.
questions.explanation: This is central to osmosis and cell boundary function.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Osmosis is the movement of water through a selectively permeable membrane from:

options.A: a dilute solution to a concentrated solution
options.B: a concentrated solution to a dilute solution
options.C: air to water only

questions.answer: Water moves from the side with more water and less solute to the side with less water and more solute.
questions.answerSolution: Water moves from the side with more water and less solute to the side with less water and more solute.
questions.explanation: Keeps osmosis at textbook level.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Which cell structure provides rigidity and strength to plant cells?

options.A: Cell membrane
options.B: Cell wall
options.C: Nucleus

questions.answer: The cell wall provides support, rigidity and protection in plant cells.
questions.answerSolution: The cell wall provides support, rigidity and protection in plant cells.
questions.explanation: The cell wall is a key difference between plant and animal cells.
questions.learningOutcome: Identifies the function of cell wall.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: A bacterial cell is called prokaryotic mainly because it:

options.A: has a large central vacuole
options.B: lacks a well-defined nucleus
options.C: has many chloroplasts

questions.answer: Bacteria lack a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
questions.answerSolution: Bacteria lack a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
questions.explanation: This checks the plant/animal/bacterial comparison in Fig. 2.10.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Which organelle is mainly associated with cellular respiration and energy release?

options.A: Mitochondrion
options.B: Cell wall
options.C: Chromoplast

questions.answer: Mitochondria are sites of cellular respiration and help release energy for the cell.
questions.answerSolution: Mitochondria are sites of cellular respiration and help release energy for the cell.
questions.explanation: This is a core organelle-function match.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Which plastid contains chlorophyll and helps plants prepare food?

options.A: Leucoplast
options.B: Chromoplast
options.C: Chloroplast

questions.answer: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and help in photosynthesis.
questions.answerSolution: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and help in photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: The question checks a direct NCERT concept.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Mitosis produces:

options.A: two identical daughter cells
options.B: four cells with half the chromosomes
options.C: only dead cells

questions.answer: Mitosis produces two daughter cells identical to the parent cell.
questions.answerSolution: Mitosis produces two daughter cells identical to the parent cell.
questions.explanation: This is stated in the chapter summary.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Meiosis is important because it produces cells that have:

options.A: twice the number of chromosomes
options.B: half the number of chromosomes
options.C: no chromosomes

questions.answer: Meiosis produces four daughter cells, each with half the number of chromosomes.
questions.answerSolution: Meiosis produces four daughter cells, each with half the number of chromosomes.
questions.explanation: This supports understanding of gamete formation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Assertion (A): The cell membrane allows only some substances to pass through it. Reason (R): The cell membrane is selectively permeable. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
questions.answerSolution: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
questions.explanation: Checks the meaning of selective permeability.
questions.learningOutcome: Connects membrane function with selective permeability.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Assertion (A): A potato piece placed in concentrated salt solution may shrink. Reason (R): Water moves out of the potato cells by osmosis. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: Water leaves the cells when the outside solution is more concentrated, so the potato may shrink.
questions.answerSolution: Water leaves the cells when the outside solution is more concentrated, so the potato may shrink.
questions.explanation: Based on the potato osmosis activity.
questions.learningOutcome: Applies osmosis to an activity observation.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Assertion (A): Plant cells usually have a rigid cell wall. Reason (R): Animals need flexible cells for movement and do not have a rigid cell wall. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: Both statements are true, but animal movement does not directly explain why plant cells have cell walls.
questions.answerSolution: Both statements are true, but animal movement does not directly explain why plant cells have cell walls.
questions.explanation: The item checks careful reasoning without being tricky.
questions.learningOutcome: Compares plant and animal cell structure.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Assertion (A): A bacterial cell lacks a well-defined nucleus. Reason (R): A bacterial cell is prokaryotic. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: Prokaryotic cells lack a well-defined nucleus.
questions.answerSolution: Prokaryotic cells lack a well-defined nucleus.
questions.explanation: This checks a key classification from Fig. 2.10.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Assertion (A): Chloroplasts help green plants make food. Reason (R): Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: Chlorophyll in chloroplasts helps capture light for photosynthesis.
questions.answerSolution: Chlorophyll in chloroplasts helps capture light for photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Directly based on plastid function.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Assertion (A): Normal cells divide in a controlled manner. Reason (R): Cancer cells lose control and divide uncontrollably. Choose the correct option.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.answer: Both statements are true, but the reason describes cancer cells rather than directly explaining normal cell control.
questions.answerSolution: Both statements are true, but the reason describes cancer cells rather than directly explaining normal cell control.
questions.explanation: Encourages careful reading of assertion-reason logic.
questions.learningOutcome: Compares normal and cancerous cell division.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why is the cell called the basic structural and functional unit of life?
questions.answer: A cell is the smallest living unit that forms the body of organisms and performs life functions.
questions.answerSolution: A cell is the smallest living unit that forms the body of organisms and performs life functions.
questions.explanation: The answer should mention both structure and function.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains why cells are basic units.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why can most cells not be studied clearly with the unaided eye?
questions.answer: Most cells are too small to be seen clearly by the unaided eye, so microscopes are needed.
questions.answerSolution: Most cells are too small to be seen clearly by the unaided eye, so microscopes are needed.
questions.explanation: Students need not give numerical limits unless asked.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Write the formula for total magnification of a compound microscope.
questions.answer: Total magnification = magnifying power of eyepiece × magnifying power of objective lens.
questions.answerSolution: Total magnification = magnifying power of eyepiece × magnifying power of objective lens.
questions.explanation: This is directly based on the textbook activity.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: What is the other name of the cell membrane?
questions.answer: The cell membrane is also called the plasma membrane.
questions.answerSolution: The cell membrane is also called the plasma membrane.
questions.explanation: Simple terminology check.
questions.learningOutcome: Recalls scientific term for cell membrane.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Define osmosis in simple words.
questions.answer: Osmosis is the movement of water through a selectively permeable membrane from a dilute solution to a concentrated solution.
questions.answerSolution: Osmosis is the movement of water through a selectively permeable membrane from a dilute solution to a concentrated solution.
questions.explanation: Accept: movement of water across selectively permeable membrane down water concentration gradient.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: What is the plant cell wall mainly made of?
questions.answer: The plant cell wall is mainly made of cellulose.
questions.answerSolution: The plant cell wall is mainly made of cellulose.
questions.explanation: Direct NCERT fact.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: What is the main function of ribosomes?
questions.answer: Ribosomes help in protein synthesis.
questions.answerSolution: Ribosomes help in protein synthesis.
questions.explanation: Teacher may accept 'making proteins'.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: What do leucoplasts store?
questions.answer: Leucoplasts store food materials such as starch, oils or proteins.
questions.answerSolution: Leucoplasts store food materials such as starch, oils or proteins.
questions.explanation: Examples like potato starch are acceptable.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: How does a large vacuole help a plant cell remain firm?
questions.answer: It stores water and maintains pressure inside the cell, helping the plant cell stay firm.
questions.answerSolution: It stores water and maintains pressure inside the cell, helping the plant cell stay firm.
questions.explanation: Connects water storage with turgidity.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why do cells divide when the body grows or repairs a wound?
questions.answer: Cells divide to produce new cells that help in growth and replace old, dead or damaged cells.
questions.answerSolution: Cells divide to produce new cells that help in growth and replace old, dead or damaged cells.
questions.explanation: This checks the chapter's wound-healing context.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Give two differences between a light microscope and an electron microscope.
questions.answer: A light microscope uses visible light and is common in school laboratories. An electron microscope uses a beam of electrons and can show much finer details at very high magnification.
questions.answerSolution: A light microscope uses visible light and is common in school laboratories. An electron microscope uses a beam of electrons and can show much finer details at very high magnification.
questions.explanation: Award marks for any two correct differences.
questions.learningOutcome: Compares tools used to study cells.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: In the potato experiment, why does the potato piece in plain water swell?
questions.answer: Plain water has more water and less solute than the cell sap. Water enters the potato cells through the selectively permeable membrane by osmosis, so the piece swells.
questions.answerSolution: Plain water has more water and less solute than the cell sap. Water enters the potato cells through the selectively permeable membrane by osmosis, so the piece swells.
questions.explanation: Look for direction of water movement and osmosis.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why does a potato piece placed in concentrated salt or sugar solution shrink?
questions.answer: The outside solution is more concentrated than the cell sap. Water moves out of the potato cells by osmosis, causing the cells and the potato piece to shrink.
questions.answerSolution: The outside solution is more concentrated than the cell sap. Water moves out of the potato cells by osmosis, causing the cells and the potato piece to shrink.
questions.explanation: Accept 'water moves out through membrane'.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why is a cell wall useful for plant cells but not present in animal cells?
questions.answer: Plants are usually fixed in one place and need rigid support to stay upright. The cell wall gives strength and protection. Animal cells lack a rigid cell wall, which allows flexibility and movement.
questions.answerSolution: Plants are usually fixed in one place and need rigid support to stay upright. The cell wall gives strength and protection. Animal cells lack a rigid cell wall, which allows flexibility and movement.
questions.explanation: This is NCERT-aligned and not beyond scope.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Name the three basic parts found in most cells and mention one function of each.
questions.answer: Cell membrane: surrounds the cell and controls movement of substances. Cytoplasm: jelly-like fluid containing organelles. Nucleus: controls cell activities and contains genetic material.
questions.answerSolution: Cell membrane: surrounds the cell and controls movement of substances. Cytoplasm: jelly-like fluid containing organelles. Nucleus: controls cell activities and contains genetic material.
questions.explanation: Award for naming and one function each.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: How is a bacterial cell different from a plant or animal cell in terms of nucleus and organelles?
questions.answer: A bacterial cell has no well-defined nucleus and lacks membrane-bound organelles, so it is prokaryotic. Plant and animal cells have a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, so they are eukaryotic.
questions.answerSolution: A bacterial cell has no well-defined nucleus and lacks membrane-bound organelles, so it is prokaryotic. Plant and animal cells have a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, so they are eukaryotic.
questions.explanation: Focus on nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Differentiate chloroplasts, chromoplasts and leucoplasts based on pigment and function.
questions.answer: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and help in photosynthesis. Chromoplasts contain coloured pigments and give colours to flowers and fruits. Leucoplasts lack pigments and store food such as starch, oils or proteins.
questions.answerSolution: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and help in photosynthesis. Chromoplasts contain coloured pigments and give colours to flowers and fruits. Leucoplasts lack pigments and store food such as starch, oils or proteins.
questions.explanation: One mark each for correct plastid type and function.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Differentiate between rough endoplasmic reticulum and smooth endoplasmic reticulum.
questions.answer: Rough ER has ribosomes on its surface and is involved in protein-related functions. Smooth ER lacks ribosomes and helps in synthesis of lipids and other materials.
questions.answerSolution: Rough ER has ribosomes on its surface and is involved in protein-related functions. Smooth ER lacks ribosomes and helps in synthesis of lipids and other materials.
questions.explanation: Keep answer at chapter level; no advanced details needed.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why are mitochondria and plastids considered special among cell organelles?
questions.answer: They have their own DNA and ribosomes and can make some of their own proteins. This makes them different from many other organelles and suggests an evolutionary connection with bacteria.
questions.answerSolution: They have their own DNA and ribosomes and can make some of their own proteins. This makes them different from many other organelles and suggests an evolutionary connection with bacteria.
questions.explanation: Accept simpler answer: own DNA and ribosomes.
questions.learningOutcome: Recognises special features of mitochondria and plastids.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 2, Ready to Go Beyond

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Write two differences between mitosis and meiosis.
questions.answer: Mitosis produces two identical daughter cells with the same number of chromosomes. Meiosis is a two-step division that produces four daughter cells, each with half the number of chromosomes.
questions.answerSolution: Mitosis produces two identical daughter cells with the same number of chromosomes. Meiosis is a two-step division that produces four daughter cells, each with half the number of chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Keep to outcomes, not detailed stages.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: A student places one potato piece in plain water and another in concentrated salt solution for one hour. The first piece becomes heavier and firmer, while the second becomes lighter and softer. Answer: (a) Name the process involved. (b) In which direction does water move in the first piece? (c) Why does the second piece become soft? (d) Name the cell structure that controls this movement.
questions.answer: (a) Osmosis. (b) Water moves into the cells. (c) Water moves out of the cells into the concentrated solution, so cells lose firmness. (d) Cell membrane/plasma membrane.
questions.answerSolution: (a) Osmosis. (b) Water moves into the cells. (c) Water moves out of the cells into the concentrated solution, so cells lose firmness. (d) Cell membrane/plasma membrane.
questions.explanation: Award 1 mark for each part.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: During a microscope activity, the visible field diameter is 4000 µm and 20 onion peel cells fit across it in a straight line. Answer: (a) Estimate the size of one cell. (b) Which instrument is being used? (c) Why is this instrument needed? (d) What does total magnification depend on?
questions.answer: (a) 4000 ÷ 20 = 200 µm. (b) Microscope/light microscope. (c) Cells are too small to see clearly with unaided eye. (d) Magnifying power of eyepiece and objective lens.
questions.answerSolution: (a) 4000 ÷ 20 = 200 µm. (b) Microscope/light microscope. (c) Cells are too small to see clearly with unaided eye. (d) Magnifying power of eyepiece and objective lens.
questions.explanation: Simple calculation and concept check.
questions.learningOutcome: Uses microscope activity to estimate cell size.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: A diagram of a plant cell shows a cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, chloroplasts, mitochondria and a large vacuole. Answer: (a) Which structure gives rigidity? (b) Which organelle helps prepare food? (c) Which organelle releases energy? (d) Which organelle stores water and helps maintain firmness?
questions.answer: (a) Cell wall. (b) Chloroplast. (c) Mitochondrion. (d) Vacuole.
questions.answerSolution: (a) Cell wall. (b) Chloroplast. (c) Mitochondrion. (d) Vacuole.
questions.explanation: Accept plural forms.
questions.learningOutcome: Identifies organelles from cell diagram clues.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: A student observes a cell diagram that has a cell membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes and genetic material, but no well-defined nucleus or membrane-bound organelles. Answer: (a) Is it likely to be prokaryotic or eukaryotic? (b) Give one reason. (c) Name one organism with such cells. (d) Are ribosomes present?
questions.answer: (a) Prokaryotic. (b) It lacks a well-defined nucleus/membrane-bound organelles. (c) Bacteria. (d) Yes.
questions.answerSolution: (a) Prokaryotic. (b) It lacks a well-defined nucleus/membrane-bound organelles. (c) Bacteria. (d) Yes.
questions.explanation: Based on bacterial cell diagram in NCERT.
questions.learningOutcome: Classifies a cell using structural features.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: A farmer adds large amounts of salt or sugar to fruits to make pickles, murabba or sharbat and store them longer. Answer: (a) Which process helps prevent microbial growth? (b) What happens to water inside bacteria or fungi in such a concentrated solution? (c) Why does this reduce spoilage? (d) Is this context within the same cell concept studied in the chapter?
questions.answer: (a) Osmosis. (b) Water moves out of the microbial cells. (c) Cells lose water, so their growth is reduced. (d) Yes, it applies osmosis through a selectively permeable membrane.
questions.answerSolution: (a) Osmosis. (b) Water moves out of the microbial cells. (c) Cells lose water, so their growth is reduced. (d) Yes, it applies osmosis through a selectively permeable membrane.
questions.explanation: Based on the chapter case question about preservation.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 2, Revise, Reflect, Refine Q16

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Describe the potato osmosis experiment and explain what it shows about the cell membrane.
questions.answer: Two similar potato pieces are weighed and placed separately in plain water and concentrated salt/sugar solution. The piece in plain water swells because water enters the cells. The piece in concentrated solution shrinks because water moves out. This shows that the cell membrane allows water to move through it and is selectively permeable.
questions.answerSolution: Two similar potato pieces are weighed and placed separately in plain water and concentrated salt/sugar solution. The piece in plain water swells because water enters the cells. The piece in concentrated solution shrinks because water moves out. This shows that the cell membrane allows water to move through it and is selectively permeable.
questions.explanation: Expected points: setup, observation in both beakers, inference about osmosis, role of membrane.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Compare plant and animal cells with respect to cell wall, plastids, vacuoles, shape and flexibility.
questions.answer: Plant cells have a cell wall, often have plastids, usually have a large central vacuole and are more rigid. Animal cells lack a cell wall and plastids, may have small vacuoles, and are more flexible in shape.
questions.answerSolution: Plant cells have a cell wall, often have plastids, usually have a large central vacuole and are more rigid. Animal cells lack a cell wall and plastids, may have small vacuoles, and are more flexible in shape.
questions.explanation: Award marks for correct comparison points.
questions.learningOutcome: Compares plant and animal cell features.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Explain the functions of any five cell organelles studied in the chapter.
questions.answer: Nucleus controls cell activities and contains genetic material. Mitochondria release energy. Ribosomes make proteins. Golgi body packs and stores materials received from ER. Lysosomes help digest foreign agents or worn-out materials. Chloroplasts help photosynthesis. Vacuoles store water and materials.
questions.answerSolution: Nucleus controls cell activities and contains genetic material. Mitochondria release energy. Ribosomes make proteins. Golgi body packs and stores materials received from ER. Lysosomes help digest foreign agents or worn-out materials. Chloroplasts help photosynthesis. Vacuoles store water and materials.
questions.explanation: Any five correct organelles with functions should receive full credit.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Why is the nucleus important for the cell? Explain using the idea that DNA controls cell structure and activities.
questions.answer: The nucleus contains genetic material/DNA. DNA carries instructions that guide cell structure, activities, growth and division. The synthetic DNA example in the chapter shows that when DNA was inserted into a cell, the cell followed instructions from the new DNA. Therefore, the nucleus is important because it stores the instructions that control the cell.
questions.answerSolution: The nucleus contains genetic material/DNA. DNA carries instructions that guide cell structure, activities, growth and division. The synthetic DNA example in the chapter shows that when DNA was inserted into a cell, the cell followed instructions from the new DNA. Therefore, the nucleus is important because it stores the instructions that control the cell.
questions.explanation: Keep answer at NCERT level; no advanced genetics required.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains control role of nucleus and DNA.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 2, Threads of Curiosity

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Explain why cell division is necessary. Include growth, repair and formation of reproductive cells in your answer.
questions.answer: Cell division produces new cells. It helps organisms grow, because growth happens mostly by increase in cell number. It repairs and replaces old, dead or damaged cells, such as during wound healing. Mitosis produces identical cells for growth and repair. Meiosis produces cells with half the chromosome number for reproduction.
questions.answerSolution: Cell division produces new cells. It helps organisms grow, because growth happens mostly by increase in cell number. It repairs and replaces old, dead or damaged cells, such as during wound healing. Mitosis produces identical cells for growth and repair. Meiosis produces cells with half the chromosome number for reproduction.
questions.explanation: Expected: growth, repair/replacement, mitosis, meiosis/gametes.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains biological importance of cell division.

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: Mitochondria and chloroplasts are important organelles in plant cells. Explain one similarity and two differences between them.
questions.answer: Similarity: both have their own DNA and ribosomes and are surrounded by membranes. Differences: mitochondria help in cellular respiration and energy release, while chloroplasts help in photosynthesis. Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll; mitochondria do not.
questions.answerSolution: Similarity: both have their own DNA and ribosomes and are surrounded by membranes. Differences: mitochondria help in cellular respiration and energy release, while chloroplasts help in photosynthesis. Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll; mitochondria do not.
questions.explanation: Paraphrased from NCERT exercise question.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 2, Revise, Reflect, Refine Q6

questions.subject: Science
questions.chapterName: Cell: The Building Block of Life
questions.question: What would happen if gametes were formed by mitosis instead of meiosis?
questions.answer: If gametes were formed by mitosis, they would have the same chromosome number as body cells instead of half. After fusion, the new cell would have double the normal chromosome number. This would disturb chromosome balance across generations. Therefore, meiosis is necessary for gamete formation.
questions.answerSolution: If gametes were formed by mitosis, they would have the same chromosome number as body cells instead of half. After fusion, the new cell would have double the normal chromosome number. This would disturb chromosome balance across generations. Therefore, meiosis is necessary for gamete formation.
questions.explanation: Paraphrased from NCERT exercise; answer should focus on chromosome number.
questions.learningOutcome: Explains why meiosis is needed for gametes.
questions.ncertReference: Class 9 Science, Chapter 2, Revise, Reflect, Refine Q15
